title: Sumitomo Chemical aims to make solid-state batteries more affordable - electrive.com
content:
Sumitomo Chemical is preparing for the series production of a new electrolyte for solid-state batteries. The halide-based material is intended to combine high performance with simpler and more cost-effective manufacturing.
Solid-state batteries promise higher energy densities and shorter charging times but have so far been considered complex and expensive to produce.
Image: Paul Scherrer Institut PSI/Mahir Dzambegovic
Sumitomo Chemical has developed a solid-electrolyte battery in collaboration with Kyoto University and Tottori University. The technology is based on halides and uses chlorine, among other elements. Sumitomo Chemical positions this material as an alternative to sulphide and oxide electrolytes. Sulphide electrolytes, in particular, are considered promising for solid-state batteries due to their high ionic conductivity. Sumitomo Chemical claims to have optimised the composition of its material to achieve comparable ionic conductivity, though the company has not yet provided specific figures.
The key advantage may lie in production. Sulphide electrolytes are sensitive to moisture and require tightly controlled manufacturing conditions. The new material is expected to be less demanding to process. According to Sumitomo Chemical, battery manufacturers could partially utilise existing lithium-ion battery production facilities and conditions. The goal is to achieve manufacturing costs on par with today’s high-performance lithium-ion batteries.
This could be particularly relevant for electric vehicles. Solid-state batteries promise higher energy density, shorter charging times, and improved safety. However, widespread adoption has so far been hindered by high costs and the challenges of scaling production. Sumitomo Chemical’s approach directly targets one of the main barriers to mass-market deployment in electric cars. The company is currently having the material evaluated by potential customers and plans to initially establish mass production in Japan. No timeline or planned production capacities have been disclosed yet.
Several companies in Japan are working towards commercialising the technology by 2027 or 2028. Toyota is collaborating with
Idemitsu Kosan on sulphide solid electrolytes
and with Sumitomo Metal Mining on cathode materials. Nissan aims to commercialise its own solid-state batteries by the fiscal year 2028.
This is increasingly giving rise to an industrial supply chain for solid-state batteries. However, Sumitomo Chemical’s halide electrolyte represents a different technological approach compared to the widely favoured sulphide systems. If the combination of high ionic conductivity, lower manufacturing requirements, and the use of existing production facilities is confirmed at an industrial scale, it could significantly reduce the cost of solid-state batteries and accelerate their mass-market adoption in electric vehicles.
about „Sumitomo Chemical aims to make solid-state batteries more affordable“

title: Cylib acquires Bavarian EV battery recycling facility - electrive.com
content:
German battery recycler Cylib has acquired a mechanical processing facility in Bavaria with capacity for up to 10,000 tonnes of end-of-life EV batteries per year. The site will supply black mass to the company's hydrometallurgical recycling plant under construction in Dormagen.
Image: Cylib
Cylib is thus expanding its operations to cover the entire battery recycling chain from end-of-life batteries to recovered raw materials. The newly purchased facility has an annual processing capacity of up to 10,000 metric tonnes of end-of-life EV batteries and is expected to begin operations in the fourth quarter of 2026, subject to approval under Germany’s Federal Immission Control Act (BImSchG).
Cylib does not name the insolvent company, but it is likely to be Roth International.
As reported in 2022
, the latter was planning to a recycling facility in Wernberg-Köblitz and recently
filed for insolvency
Cyclib does confirm that the Bavarian plant will mechanically process end-of-life EV batteries into black mass, which will then be supplied to Cyclib’s hydrometallurgical recycling facility currently under construction in Dormagen, North Rhine-Westphalia. There, the material will be refined into battery-grade raw materials including lithium, graphite, nickel, cobalt and manganese.
The company says repurposing an existing industrial site reduces both the environmental impact and the time required to bring new recycling capacity online. Cylib expects to gradually ramp up operations in cooperation with authorities, equipment suppliers and customers, with full capacity targeted by the end of 2027.
Image: Cylib
Image: Cylib
Image: Cylib
“Cylib is built on a clear conviction: battery recycling has to be end-to-end, from the end-of-life battery to the high-quality critical raw material,” said Dr Lilian Schwich, Co-CEO and Co-Founder of cylib. “In our pilot plant we have demonstrated those end-to-end capabilities. In Dormagen we are building the high-quality material recovery end. With this acquisition we are now adding the end-of-life processing capability at large scale.”
The acquisition was completed through insolvency proceedings and includes the processing line, building and property. Cylib said it will recommission the previously decommissioned site under its own operational, environmental and safety standards while assuming full responsibility for its operation.
According to the company, the site gives Cylib direct access to Germany’s automotive manufacturing region and battery supply chains in southern Germany and Central Europe. It will also serve as the first node in the company’s collection and pre-processing network, securing feedstock for the Dormagen plant.
“Building large-scale processing capacity allows us to offer our customers and partners, including OEMs, tier-one suppliers, and battery manufacturers, even greater value and a more comprehensive service,” said Dr Gideon Schwich, Co-CEO and Co-Founder of cylib. “This acquisition is another step forward on our long-term roadmap. Bringing this site back into responsible operation is a task our team is fully committed to.”
about „Cylib acquires Bavarian EV battery recycling facility“

title: Study: Kia e-Niro leads battery health ranking after high mileage - electrive.com
content:
A study by Swedish used EV retailer Carla suggests modern electric cars retain most of their battery capacity even after high mileage. Based on almost 10,000 battery tests, the analysis also highlights differences between models, battery chemistries and suppliers.
The ranking forms part of Carla’s Electric Vehicle Battery Study 2026. It compares average battery health across individual electric car models after more than 100,000 kilometres of driving. A total of 9,954 battery tests were carried out for the analysis in Sweden between 2022 and 2026 using diagnostics from Austrian battery specialist AVILOO. Carla says the dataset is one of the largest collections of battery health data from the Swedish used EV market.
Only models with a ‘sufficiently large number of battery tests’ were included to ensure meaningful comparisons, the EV retailer states.
The Kia e-Niro with the 64 kWh battery achieved the highest average battery health at 97.25%, followed by the Hyundai Kona Electric (64 kWh) at 97.18% and the Kia EV6 (77.4 kWh) at 95.95%.
Other leading models include the Volvo XC40 Recharge with the 69 kWh CATL battery at 94.70%, the Polestar 2 with the 78 kWh CATL battery at 94.35% and the BMW i3 with the 120 Ah battery at 93.77%. Tesla’s highest-ranked model was the Model 3 with the 60.5 kWh CATL LFP battery, recording 93.34%. The top 20 also includes the Audi e-tron 50 and 55, Skoda Enyaq iV, Volkswagen ID.3 and ID.4, Tesla Model S, Model X and Model Y, and the Audi Q4 e-tron.
According to Carla, several of the highest-ranked models still retain between 95% and 97% of their original battery capacity after more than 100,000 kilometres. The company says this indicates lower degradation than many buyers expect and supports the view that modern EV batteries generally offer long service lives.
The study also found that battery chemistry and cell supplier can significantly influence long-term battery health. Comparing different battery versions of the Tesla Model 3, Carla found that the variant with CATL’s LFP batteries retained an average of 93.3% battery health, ahead of versions using LG Chem cells at 91.5% and Panasonic batteries at 89.8% and 88.2%, depending on the battery pack.
Carla says the findings highlight the value of battery testing when assessing used electric vehicles. While battery condition should be considered alongside mileage, age and equipment, the company concludes that many of the most common EVs on the Swedish used car market continue to show high battery health after years of operation.
Further analyses covering battery health by brand, degradation over mileage and the prevalence of severely degraded battery packs are due to be published later this summer.
(in Swedish)
about „Study: Kia e-Niro leads battery health ranking after high mileage“

title: NatPower, Tesla set sights on 100GWh+ in BESS deal for Europe – Batteries International
content:
Energy Storage, Lithium, News
NatPower, Tesla set sights on 100GWh+ in BESS deal for Europe
–  July 21, 2026 09:38 am BST
Deal includes initial 25GWh+ of BESS capacity
Energy projects developer NatPower and Tesla have agreed a multi-year deal targeting the potential construction of more than 100GWh of BESS systems across European markets.
An initial five projects under the deal are planned for Italy and the UK — covering more than 25GWh of capacity — to be owned and operated by NatPower.
Tesla will provide its Megapack battery tech in addition to other services, including financial, engineering, procurement and construction, under the terms of the agreement announced on June 23.
Aggregate construction value across the full scope of the agreement is estimated at $4-5 billion and revenues expected from projects is set to exceed $15 billion over 20 years.
The partners said BESS systems will be delivered and allocated through a coordinated system that directly links production, financing and execution on a multi-country basis — claimed to be the first time at this scale — “moving beyond an opportunistic and local approach”.
Key areas of focus include manufacturing capacity reservation, grid access and connection, permitting and regulatory compliance, financial structure and execution scheduling and timeline management.
“The agreement goes beyond the supply of technology alone, establishing an integrated structure combining electrical infrastructure, industrial capacity and advanced energy trading models, which provide the flexibility and certainty required to finance and deliver projects of NatPower’s scale.”
NatPower manages its operations from five main offices across Asia, Europe and the US. CEO Fabrizio Zago said his company and Tesla had established an ecosystem that enables alignment between capital and execution, and that can be replicated across multiple markets.

title: Rich Botts and Jessica Parnell join Eagle Oxide – Batteries International
content:
Lead, News, People
Rich Botts and Jessica Parnell join Eagle Oxide
–  July 21, 2026 09:36 am BST
Rich Botts has been appointed director of operations at Eagle Oxide Services, the company announced on July 17.
The announcement came after Eagle announced on July 1 that Jessica Parnell had joined the firm as project manager.
Botts, who has more than three decades of experience in lead acid battery manufacturing and maintenance, was most recently with Hammond Lead Products and previously at Pratt Industries.
Eagle said his background includes extensive experience with Barton oxide systems, ball mills, red lead and litharge production, combustion systems, melt pots, hammer mills, and both mechanical and pneumatic material handling systems.
He also holds technical certifications in welding and electrical work, giving him a practical, hands-on perspective that complements his leadership experience.
Meanwhile, Jessica Parnell will help manage customer projects from planning through execution, supporting the company’s commitment to quality, innovation, and on-time delivery.
Eagle said Parnell brings more than 18 years of manufacturing and project leadership experience from Honda’s car production facility in Indiana.
Throughout her career, she has led project, production, and quality teams of more than 200 employees while helping oversee the successful launch of new vehicle programmes.
Parnell’s experience includes managing new model vehicle weld programs and serving as technical-stage project lead for Honda’s R&D team, where she played a key role in coordinating new vehicle development.
Eagle said that her background in complex manufacturing environments and cross-functional project management is a natural fit for the company as it continues to expand its global customer base.
On Botts, Eagle CEO Eric Holtan, said: “Rich understands this industry from the ground up. His experience and technical knowledge make him an excellent addition to our leadership team, and we’re excited about the value he’ll bring to Eagle and our customers.”
Holtan said Parnell’s experience leading large-scale manufacturing projects and driving collaboration across multiple teams will strengthen the firm’s ability to execute projects efficiently and continue providing exceptional service to its customers.

title: 7 Battery-Powered Machines Transforming Construction & Mining
content:
July 21, 2026
Start slideshow
Hitachi’s Full Battery Dump Truck running while charging
Hitachi Construction Machinery
For more than a century, diesel engines have powered excavators, haul trucks, and loaders on construction sites. Diesel-based engines offered the torque, range, and refueling speed that heavy equipment needed, so mine operators built entire sites around this power source. Unfortunately, operators’ reliance on diesel created many drawbacks. Diesel exhaust filled underground tunnels with heat and particulates, engine noise limited work near hospitals and schools, and fuel became one of the largest recurring expenses on any job site or mine.
Battery technology has advanced significantly enough in recent years to challenge diesel’s superiority among heavy machinery. Lithium-ion and lithium-iron-phosphate battery packs now deliver enough power density to move hundred-ton machines. At the same time, megawatt-scale chargers and battery-swapping systems reduce the downtime that once made electrification impractical for continuous mining operations. Equipment makers have also begun retrofitting mine infrastructure with trolley lines and dedicated charging posts to support these larger battery packs.
Navigating the Hybrid Shift
Over the last few years, heavy equipment manufacturers have developed and deployed battery-powered machinery to customers around the world. Fortescue, BHP, and Rio Tinto have all begun field trials of battery-electric haul trucks in Australia's Pilbara region, while Hitachi demonstrated a full battery dump truck to mining customers at a Zambian copper mine. Now, manufacturers are moving from single-machine trials to fleet-scale orders as mine operators pursue emissions targets set for the end of the decade.
We've put together a list of seven battery-powered machines transforming construction and mining sites. Read on to learn about the haul trucks, excavators, and loaders leading that change.

title: Startups Pitch Investors to Transform the Battery Industry
content:
Startup pitches at NSF Investor Day showcase battery innovations driving sustainability, high-performance materials, and domestic supply chain resilience.
July 20, 2026
Investor Day spotlights the next generation of energy storage startups emerging from the NSF Energy Storage Engine in Upstate New York ecosystem.
Courtesy of Maria Guerra.
Dr. Fernando Gomez, Director of Translational Entrepreneurship at NSF Energy Storage Engine, welcomed attendees to Cornell Tech, saying, “Welcome to Cornell Tech. For those of you who are here for the first time, I hope you enjoy the beautiful campus…If you're an investor, you're going to see amazing companies pitch, and I hope you go and talk to them, understand their businesses.”
Meera Sampat, CEO of the NSF Energy Storage Engine, followed with an overview of the engine’s mission to accelerate battery innovation, strengthen domestic manufacturing, and drive economic
growth in upstate New York
. She highlighted key achievements, including mobilizing 60 partners, funding 15 startups, and raising $20M in follow-on funding. “Together, all of us here in this room have the opportunity to build America's battery tech capital right here in New York,” she emphasized.
Investor Day
featured a showcase of 13 startups, each with 7 minutes to pitch their groundbreaking technologies. From advanced anode materials to sustainable recycling solutions, the event offered a glimpse of the future of energy storage. As Sampat noted, “These startups are not just solving today’s challenges—they’re building the foundation for a more sustainable and energy-efficient tomorrow.”
Breaking Down the Limits of Solid-State Battery Performance
The event underscored three major trends driving the future of energy storage:
Sustainability
: Startups like Amel Energy and Bridge Green Upcycle are prioritizing environmentally friendly solutions, addressing the urgent need for sustainable materials and processes.
High-performance materials
: Companies such as Tyfast Energy and Elementium Materials are pushing the boundaries of battery performance, enabling faster charging, longer lifespans, and greater energy density.
Domestic supply chain resilience
: With a focus on U.S.-based manufacturing and sourcing, startups like Fermi Energy and Molhill Inc. are strengthening the domestic battery supply chain to reduce reliance on foreign materials.
1. Battery manufacturing with PFAS-free binder:
Derrick Maxwell, PhD, CEO of
, delivered a pitch that addressed one of the most pressing challenges in battery manufacturing, which is the reliance on toxic solvents and PFAS-based materials. “Most of us don’t realize that the batteries we carry around every day are made with hazardous chemicals,” Maxwell began, highlighting the environmental and regulatory risks associated with traditional processes and the importance of removing toxic solvents and PFAS in cathode manufacturing.
Learn How an iPhone Battery Is Manufactured
Derrick Maxwell, PhD, CEO of Amel Energy. Courtesy of Maria Guerra.
Amel Energy’s innovative solution replaces these harmful materials with a non-toxic, biofermented solvent derived from corn and a
PFAS-free binder
, both sourced domestically. This approach not only avoids regulatory hurdles but also offers significant cost savings—up to 2x for the solvent and 10x for the binder. Maxwell emphasized the scalability of their solution, noting that it’s a true drop-in replacement that requires no changes to existing manufacturing equipment. “We’re designing this platform to be compatible with all cathode materials and conductive carbons, ensuring seamless adoption for manufacturers,” he explained.
Having already achieved Technology Readiness Level (TRL) 5, Amel Energy is now targeting TRL 7 or 8 as they expand their R&D operations in New York State. With two pending patents, successful pilot tests, and over $900,000 in grants, Amel Energy is targeting the US defense industry as a beachhead market; the company projects significant cost reductions for gigafactories. Amel Energy is seeking $3 million in seed funding to scale operations, build out their team, and further develop their innovative solutions, with a vision to transform battery manufacturing into a sustainable and cost-effective industry.
Video Explores Why US Battery Manufacturing Lags Behind China—And What It Will Take to Catch Up
2. High-performance batteries for heavy-duty platforms
: GJ la O', CEO and Co-founder of
, introduced the company’s lithium vanadium oxide (LVO) anode technology, which aims to address the limitations of traditional lithium-ion batteries in heavy-duty applications. Designed for industries like mining and defense, Tyfast’s batteries are engineered to handle high power demands, extreme conditions, and continuous operation.
Tyfast is using LVO anode technology. Courtesy of Maria Guerra.
A key innovation is the addition of vanadium to the anode. “Vanadium oxide-based chemistries have five times lower expansion than graphite, faster lithium transport, and operate at a higher voltage,” la O’ explained. These properties result in longer cycle life, faster charge and discharge rates, and improved safety, even in extreme temperatures. According to the CEO, in mining, the technology enables electric trucks to operate 24/7, saving over $1 million annually in fuel costs, while in defense, it supports rapid charging and high-power discharge for applications like directed energy systems.
Tyfast has secured $3 million in private funding and $12 million in federal and state awards, with three patents granted. The company is raising $10 million to establish an industrial pilot line and advance toward commercialization. “We’re building a novel technology to electrify the toughest jobs on the planet,” la O’ concluded.
Startups like the ones participating in the NSF Energy Storage Engine’s Investor Day at Cornell Tech exemplify the groundbreaking advancements in sustainability, high-performance materials, and domestic supply chain resilience, underscoring the critical role these trends will play in shaping the future of energy storage.
These innovations, combined with the collective efforts of the NSF and its partners, are driving progress toward a more sustainable, energy-efficient, and resilient future. As New York State solidifies its position as a leader in battery technology, the NSF’s commitment to fostering innovation and economic growth will play a crucial role in advancing the battery industry within the state while simultaneously strengthening the US domestic battery supply chain.
Maria Guerra
Senior Editor-Battery Technology, Informa Markets
Maria L. Guerra is a Senior Editor at
Battery Technology
, blending her engineering background with a passion for technology journalism. She holds a Bachelor of Science in Electrical Engineering from Universidad Metropolitana in Caracas, Venezuela, and a Master of Science in Electrical Engineering from NYU Tandon School of Engineering.
Guerra's career spans power systems engineering, oil & gas consulting, and technical writing and editing. She worked for several years at Kellogg, Brown & Root Ltd. (KBR) in the UK, conducting power system studies and providing technical support for offshore and onshore projects. During her internship at Pequiven S.A. in Venezuela, she trained neural networks using MATLAB (Kohonen Map) to estimate electrical circuit variables for oil wells (e.g., voltage, current, and power).
In addition to her engineering roles, Guerra worked as a freelance technical editor, refining complex technical content for clarity and accuracy. She later became a Power/Analog Editor at
Electronic Design
, covering advancements in the semiconductor market, focusing on power/analog electronics systems.
Battery Technology
, Guerra now delivers in-depth coverage of battery manufacturing, EV advancements, energy storage systems, and the evolving landscape of critical minerals and second-life batteries. She is passionate about uncovering the stories that shape the future of electrification, from cutting-edge battery innovations to policy shifts driving sustainable energy.
Guerra is also a member of
The Battery Show North America Advisory Board
, where she contributes her expertise to advising on industry trends and speaker selection for The Battery Show North America, while helping to shape discussions on battery technology and electrification. She was part of the award-winning Battery Technology team recognized with a
Neal Award for Best Coverage of the 2024 Election
Neal Award for Best Explanatory Content
in 2026 for her coverage of battery breakthroughs.
Maria Guerra is based in Brooklyn, NY, and can be reached at
[email protected]

title: Delta & Shell Aviation Sign 5-Year SAF Supply Chain Deal
content:
Delta & Shell Aviation Sign 5-Year SAF Supply Chain Deal
Prioritise Us
Delta Air Lines and Shell Aviation forge five-year partnership to scale sustainable aviation fuel infrastructure across five major US airports
Delta Air Lines and Shell Aviation have entered a five-year agreement to expand sustainable aviation fuel distribution across multiple US airports through 2030. The collaboration targets the creation of infrastructure and logistics networks needed to scale sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) supply chains at commercial airports.
According to the companies,
sustainable aviation fuel
can cut emissions by 80% compared to traditional jet fuel.
The partnership between Shell Aviation and Delta will focus on building the operational capacity to transport, blend and distribute SAF across five major US hubs.
The agreement covers SAF supply operations at Los Angeles International Airport, Portland International Airport, John F Kennedy International Airport, Logan International Airport and Minneapolis-St Paul International Airport.
Shell Aviation will manage the logistics of transporting both neat and blended SAF to these facilities.
The companies plan to establish infrastructure for long-term SAF supply at each location. This could mean constructing blending facilities, storage systems and delivery mechanisms at airport sites.
"This collaboration delivers on today's fuel needs and tomorrow's aviation solutions," says Reema Bari, Head of Aviation Americas at Shell. "By supplying conventional jet, SAF and longer-term innovation, the deal will help strengthen energy security and contribute to the
transformation of aviation
The partnership builds on prior work between the two organisations. Delta has increased its SAF procurement to more than 23 million gallons in 2025.
Shell Aviation will handle the movement of SAF from production facilities to airport locations. The company is working to solidify logistics, blending and
distribution capabilities
for what it describes as dependable supply.
This is about activating real supply chains at scale and creating a model that others can build on as we work across the industry to expand lower-impact travel
Amelia DeLuca, Chief Sustainability Officer at Delta
The approach includes transporting neat SAF and managing blending operations at distribution points. This could show how fuel supply chains must adapt to accommodate alternative aviation fuels alongside conventional jet fuel.
"Current instability and uncertainty have made one thing very clear to consumers and businesses alike, supply diversity matters," says Amelia DeLuca, Delta's Chief Sustainability Officer.
"With Shell, we're proving that scaling SAF isn't theoretical, it's achievable."
"This is about activating real supply chains at scale and creating a model that others can build on as we work across the industry to expand lower-impact travel," Amelia adds.
Shell has invested in SAF production through partnerships with companies including EcoOils and LanzaJet. In addition to this, the company has secured long-term supply agreements with producers such as Montana Renewables and World Energy.
The Avelia platform, which uses
blockchain technology
, is part of Shell's infrastructure for managing SAF supply chains. The system could mean improved tracking of fuel sourcing and distribution across multiple supply chain participants.
Delta and Shell are evaluating next-generation SAF production pathways including alcohol-to-jet and power-to-liquid technologies. These production methods could require different logistics and distribution systems than current SAF variants.
The partnership combines Shell's fuel production and infrastructure operations with Delta's
procurement and operational requirements
. According to the companies, collaboration across the aviation value chain is necessary to commercialise SAF at scale.
Chief Sustainability Officer
Head of Aviation

title: Nintex: Using AI and Automation for Supply Chain Compliance
content:
Nintex: Using AI and Automation for Supply Chain Compliance
Prioritise Us
Nintex has published a whitepaper, exploring the risk of unauthorised AI use and the need for greater compliance to ensure supply chain safety
Supply chains around the world have been transformed by AI – ten years ago, processes were all manual, relying on spreadsheets and workers handling a steady stream of urgent but repetitive tasks.
However, ongoing volatility and supply chain disruption has meant that these systems no longer work. As supply chains began finding themselves in a constant state of reaction, with changing regulations and complex relationship demands, other processes were needed.
AI entered the industry to transform efficiency, handling the repetitive and time-consuming tasks so the human workforce could focus on relationship-building and decision-making.
However, there have been concerns surrounding the acceleration of
and the increasing risk of data
attacks and security risks
A recent whitepaper by Nintex explores how enterprise automation is able to help drive responsible AI adoption, with a control over sensitive data and strong risk-management.
To find out more, read
the whitepaper
The technology is moving faster than people and organisations can adapt.
Keith Payne, APAC Regional Vice President at Nintex
Nintex is a process automation platform aiming to help businesses streamline workflows. In the era of AI adoption, but also AI fears, Nintex has published an enterprise guide, exploring how businesses can implement adoption without sacrificing supply chain control.
A common discovery that platforms are finding within businesses is that organisations are quick to implement AI and
automation across their workflows
, but they often fail to do so accurately. They are either applying it to the wrong software, focusing on the wrong tasks, using it as 'one size fits all model', or putting their enterprise at risk by not taking the proper precautions.
"It feels different with AI. It’s moving quickly. The pace is unprecedented. The technology is moving faster than people and organisations can adapt," Keith Payne, APAC Regional Vice President at Nintex told Forbes Australia.
What we then see is greater risk of cyber attacks and data breaches, complications to supply chain workflow, or higher costs without the benefit. Nintex aims to fix that with its whitepaper, exploring where organisations go wrong with their integration of the software and how businesses can accurately use it to see gains.
Core takeaways from the whitepaper are:
The report explores how organisations are being impacted by AI, stating, "AI can have as many cons as pros, unless you set the stage for your AI initiatives with automation built for enterprises".
To counteract the cons, Nintex uses its platform to future-proof
automation investments
, making sure it is governable and can respond to ongoing AI advancements. AI tools which cannot adapt mean that businesses who invest in them, will soon need to spend more money on modern tools. Nintex argues that flexibility is the key to staying secure when focusing on supply chain security.
Report findings
90% of employees already use AI tools, often without approval
40% of organisations have purchased LLM subscriptions
51% of enterprises use 100-300 SaaS solutions
Nintex reports that unauthorised use of AI is more widespread than employers realise – as employees look at ways to speed up processes, they are not waiting for subscriptions from their bosses. Instead, they are creating a visibility issue.
Leaders are no longer in control of the data, meaning there is no standardisation across operations. This puts the company at risk of inefficient privacy settings, too much data-sharing and more.
As a result, Nintex calls for a new strategy across enterprise automation, focusing on consistency and governance in order to protect organisation data and unlock full value from automation. This includes the automation of compliance features, such as audit trails, standardised data handling and embedded workforce rules.
Organisations need to ensure control and compliance of AI use within the
supply chain workflow
, in order to ensure connectivity and visibility throughout operations. This helps strengthen processes across the workflow, resulting in more resilience.
To find out more, read
the whitepaper

title: UK Govt & BBB Target £100m Aerospace Supply Chain Gap
content:
UK Govt & BBB Target £100m Aerospace Supply Chain Gap
Prioritise Us
The British Business Bank and UK Government are advancing plans for a £100m debt fund to help high-potential aerospace suppliers scale production
With order backlogs across the global aerospace sector reaching record highs, mid-tier manufacturers are finding conditions testing. The demand for next-generation aircraft is high, but securing the capital required to build production capacity remains difficult.
In response to this situation, the British Business Bank (BBB) has submitted a proposal to the UK Government to launch a £100m (US$131m)
aerospace supply chain debt fund
The initiative aims to bring together public funding, private capital and major industry original equipment manufacturers (OEMs), including Airbus, Rolls-Royce, GKN Aerospace and the trade association ADS.
Key Facts: £100m Aerospace Debt Fund
Fund Target & Structure: A proposed £100m (US$131m) debt fund blending public and private capital to provide non-dilutive working capital for established UK suppliers.
Strategic Partners: Developed by the British Business Bank alongside the UK Government, ADS, Airbus, Rolls-Royce, and GKN Aerospace.
Core Objective: Closes the scale-up finance gap by funding equipment, tooling and facility expansions needed long before contract revenues start.
Policy Alignment: Complements the UK Industrial Strategy, the Aerospace Growth Partnership (AGP) and existing R&D grants like the £975m (US$1.28bn) Aerospace Technology Institute (ATI) scheme.
If approved, the proposed fund will address the scale-up finance gap that could prevent Tier 2 and Tier 3 suppliers from expanding their output to meet global demand.
One of the biggest causes of cash flow friction for the mid-tier aerospace suppliers looking to scale up is the long programme development cycles necessary to do so. Upfront investments in specialised machinery, facility upgrades, tooling,
workforce upskilling
and stringent qualification processes must often be made years before revenue flows from prime contractors.
This means the length of time capital needs to be secured for is often extensive, and traditional commercial lenders can be reluctant to extend debt over these timelines. This leaves competitive suppliers undercapitalised at the exact moment OEMs demand higher delivery rates.
The suggested £100m (US$131m) fund will specifically be structured as a debt vehicle and not equity capital, which will allow established suppliers with secure contract pipelines to access
structured working capital
without diluting ownership or putting pressure on operational balance sheets.
“The continued success of the UK's aerospace sector depends on the capacity, resilience and competitiveness of the supply chain that sits behind it,” says Louis Taylor, CEO of the British Business Bank.
“This fund will help strengthen the aerospace supply chain by giving high-potential UK suppliers the capital they need to scale, invest and compete for future aircraft work.”
The funding proposition aligns with the Government's broader Industrial Strategy and the ambitions of the Aerospace Growth Partnership.
The combination of state backing and private capital will help to shore up domestic sub-tiers against both international competition and
supply chain disruptions
Louis Taylor, CEO of the British Business Bank
With direct involvement from organisations such as Airbus and Rolls-Royce, it shows that aerospace supply chain decision-makers also acknowledge that an OEM’s production rate is only as fast as its most constrained sub-tier supplier.
“By bringing together public and private sector investment, we can support a strategically important sector, back
high-value manufacturing jobs
and help more UK businesses play a larger role in global aerospace supply chains," Louis adds.
However, the proposal arrives during a broader political shift in the UK following the arrival of a new Prime Minister. Andy Burnham officially took office this week with a view of bringing the “biggest changes” to politics in 40 years, but industry leaders remain uncertain about his long-term policy commitments or stance on specific industrial investments.
As the UK Government and the BBB finalise the fund’s exact criteria and delivery routes, the focus for UK suppliers turns to speed-to-market. Non-dilutive scale-up capital could prove to be the critical catalyst required to convert rising aircraft demand into
, high-volume production.
Prime Minister of the United Kingdom
Chair, British Business Investment Services Ltd (SMF9)

Prioritise Us
Ahead of Supply Chain LIVE: The London Summit, we speak to Süleyman Nezih Kocaman, who will be sharing insights at our Supply Chain Management panel
Supply chain management has changed over the years, as leaders are expected to do more with less and macroeconomic trends have squeezed organisations around the world.
At Supply Chain LIVE: The London Summit, leaders will gather to share insights about the current state of global operations, exploring how AI and sustainability have had their impact and how organisations can best avoid risk.
Join more than 1,000 attendees and learn from more than 50 speakers as the event explores some of the biggest supply chain challenges and most innovative solutions.
To make sure you do not miss out,
secure your ticket today
Ahead of the event, Supply Chain Digital spoke to one of the experts who will be speaking on the
Supply Chain Management panel
Süleyman Nezih Kocaman is the Global Supply Chain Networks & Manufacturing Excellence Sr. Director at pladis Global.
In his role, he helps shape the global manufacturing and supply chain strategy across a network of production sites and supply chain operations spanning multiple regions. His main focus is on ensuring that the company's manufacturing networks are
resilient, efficient
and capable of supporting long-term growth while continuously improving performance, productivity and service to its consumers.
Throughout his career, he has worked across supply chain, manufacturing, logistics, customer service and transformation roles, and he is passionate about connecting strategy with operational execution.
What excites me most is the opportunity to learn from and engage with other supply chain leaders who are facing many of the same challenges and opportunities that we are seeing across the industry. Supply chains today operate in an environment of unprecedented complexity, whether that's geopolitical uncertainty, changing consumer expectations, sustainability requirements, or the rapid development of
digital technologies
Events like this create a valuable space where each of us can move beyond theory and discuss what is actually working in practice. I am particularly looking forward to exchanging experiences, hearing different perspectives and discussing how organisations are building more agile, resilient and digitally enabled supply chains for the future.
I'll be sharing my perspective on the future of supply chain management and how leaders can navigate an increasingly volatile environment. We'll discuss how digital technologies,
AI and operational excellence
are helping organisations build more agile and resilient supply chains, while also improving service, sustainability and overall business performance.
For me, the key challenge is no longer just managing disruption – it's creating supply chains that can continuously adapt and thrive in a changing world.
Supply chains have become one of the most strategic functions within any organisation. Over the last few years, we've seen how disruptions can quickly impact customers, businesses and entire industries. At the same time, supply chains are being asked to deliver more than ever before—greater resilience, lower costs, improved sustainability, faster response times and stronger customer service.
Because of this, supply chain management is no longer just about moving products from point A to point B. It is about making smarter decisions, balancing competing priorities, and creating
competitive advantage
. Discussions like these are important because they help organisations learn from one another, challenge conventional thinking, and identify new ways to improve performance.

title: Risk Ledger: Navigating Supply Chain Risk With £24M Funding
content:
Risk Ledger: Navigating Supply Chain Risk With £24M Funding
Prioritise Us
Risk Ledger has secured a significant investment in Series B funding, unlocking new opportunities to expand its customer network and better integrate AI
Supply chain security platform, Risk Ledger, has received £24m (US$32.3m) in Series B funding.
, led by Axiom Equity, will help the company bring greater unification to organisations around the world, a critical feature in today's volatile market.
We built the company on one conviction: organisations are stronger when they Defend-as-One, sharing intelligence and reducing risk together rather than in isolation.
Haydn Brooks, CEO and Co-founder of Risk Ledger
Risk Ledger is a third-party risk management platform which aims to revolutionise supply chain security through its
unified solutions
. It onboards and connects entire supply chains into an active network, bringing cohesion to complexity. The platform helps identify potential threats and risks to supply chain integrity with its real–time insights.
In recent years, supply chains around the world have faced unprecedented levels of complexity and risk, driven by a range of geopolitical and macroeconomic pressures. Ongoing wars, changing trade relationships and inflation have all had their impact on global supply chains.
As a result, there has been an increase in supply chain cyber attacks, impacting profits, economies and livelihoods. In 2025, attacks on major UK retailers, as well as JLR impacted production and profit significantly. For JLR, production in its UK sites was shut down for weeks, meaning staff could not go to work and backlogs for vehicles were created.
As supply chain cyber attacks become more regular and more malicious, organisations need to gain modern preventative measures. Businesses around the world have been relying on old systems, which are unable to keep up with the demands and complexity of today. The tools were built for a more simplistic time, where third-party relationships had less volatility within them and AI was not even a factor.
The legacy tool assesses suppliers in a singular fashion, analysing them in isolation –
modern supply chains
act as living organisms, with a constantly changing flow. As such, a risk management tool needs to be able to constantly monitor something which is changing. Risk Ledger aims to fill that gap.
"When we started Risk Ledger, third-party risk was something every company managed on its own, and collaboration across supplier ecosystems within sectors was rare," explains Haydn Brooks, CEO and Co-founder of Risk Ledger.
"We built the company on one conviction: organisations are stronger when they Defend-as-One, sharing intelligence and reducing risk together rather than in isolation. That conviction now connects more than 16,000 organisations. This investment lets us build that vision faster, extending collective defence to more customers, putting AI to work on the manual tasks that consume security teams and bringing Active Supply Chain Security to the United States.”
Founded in 2018, Risk Ledger has uses its vast and trusted supplier network to streamline risk management processes and reduce manual workload. It offers real-time, actionable data which is collected directly from suppliers, in order to identify, measure and manage
third-party risks
Now, in a Series B funding round led by Axiom Equity, it has raised £24m (US$32.3m), which it will use to help organisations transform their risk assessment capabilities, with a movement towards Active Supply Chain Security. Axiom led the funding round, alongside Mercia Ventures, a returning investor.
Through this investment, Risk Ledger aims to grow its UK customer network, expand into the US market and grow its supply chain security tools. Already, more than 16,000 organisations use its network, including critical sectors such as insurance, infrastructure, government and
financial services
. The investments by Axiom and Mercia Ventures demonstrate the demand for a platform such as this.
“Risk Ledger is creating a category rather than competing in an old one," says Jonathan Organ, Founding Partner, of Axiom Equity.
"The network it has built is hard to replicate and grows more valuable with every organisation that joins, which is exactly the kind of business we look to back. The team has earned real trust with serious customers, and the product reflects that discipline. We are pleased to lead this round as the final investment from our first fund, and to support Risk Ledger through its next stage of growth.”
Recent cyber attacks
M&S was shut down for six weeks in April 2025 following a cyber attack
In April 2025, the Co-op saw shortages and payment issues due to attacks
Operations at JLR sites across Solihull, Halewood and Wolverhampton were halted following attacks
20,000 Instagram users faced a data breach in June 2026
There was an attempted US$25m extortion attempt on Novo Nordisk in June 2026
Risk Ledger uses a network-first approach in its platform. Each supplier completes an assessment and is asked to alter it across the network in real-time, using a single and current platform, rather than using repeated questionnaires which can quickly become outdated. Each organisation can see the assessment in real-time, with a growing visibility as more organisations join the network.
The company uses this to form its Active Supply Chain Security focus – it is a continuous and collaborative format for managing risk, encouraging a Defend-as-One unit.
"We backed Risk Ledger at Series A because we believed Haydn and the team had identified a fundamentally better way to manage
supply chain cyber risk
. Three years on, that conviction has only strengthened," adds Adam Lovell, Investor at Mercia Ventures.
"Three years on, that conviction has only strengthened. The team, customer base and the quality of the product all speak for themselves. We are delighted to welcome Axiom as lead investor and excited to see what the business can achieve with the capital and operational support this round provides."
The Series B funding is enabling the company to expand its operations more – which is particularly critical in today's environment. The use of AI in supply chains has been vital in ensuring organisations can bypass manual and time-consuming tasks, but it has also exposed more vulnerable parties to cyber attacks.
Risk Ledger is using AI tools and its platforms to unlock new generations of tools which are built with an expansive network in mind, and this will reveal risks at a more precise level. It will also be expanding into the US in order to meet the pressure on supply chains before it combusts.
Co-Founder & CEO
Founding Partner

title: Bain Capital Secures SupplyOn to Advance Manufacturing
content:
Bain Capital Secures SupplyOn to Advance Manufacturing
Prioritise Us
Global investment firm Bain Capital expands its industrial network with the strategic acquisition of a leading European supply chain platform SupplyOn
Bain Capital has announced that it has acquired a majority stake in SupplyOn, a prominent European digital supply chain network for complex manufacturing. With this acquisition, the investment firm aims to leverage its operational expertise and resources to accelerate the international expansion of the business.
The shares purchased by Bain Capital were from existing automotive and industrial shareholders, including Bosch, Continental, Schaeffler and ZF. These companies will continue to maintain a close commercial relationship with the platform moving forward.
This latest announcement follows on from Bain Capital completing its acquisition of Everllence earlier in the year. That transaction expanded the presence of the investment firm within the advanced engineering sector.
Key facts to know about the acquisition
Bain Capital is acquiring a majority stake through its Tech Opportunities business in Europe.
Existing industrial shareholders will retain a significant minority stake in the company.
Capital from the deal will fund new investments into platform software and artificial intelligence capabilities.
The transaction remains subject to customary regulatory approvals and is expected to close before the end of 2026.
These consecutive acquisitions demonstrate a focused effort to integrate
digital supply chain solutions
with physical manufacturing infrastructure. Leaders in the sector are increasingly looking for ways to bridge the gap between software and hardware operations.
SupplyOn manages complex cross-border supply chains for critical industries such as automotive,
and railway manufacturing, which are sectors that require high levels of transparency to prevent production delays.
Bain Capital says it has “deep experience investing in European technology and industrials businesses” and this capital injection will help the platform scale operations into new geographical markets.
James Stevens, a Partner on Bain Capital's Tech Opportunities team, explains: “SupplyOn represents a rare combination of product quality, network strength, high customer advocacy and market leadership in
supply chain coordination
across Europe.
“The company has built a deeply embedded platform that customers rely on for day-to-day operations. We see real runway to expand into adjacent sectors and invest in product capabilities that matter to manufacturers and suppliers alike.”
The investment will focus on upgrading the user interface and incorporating advanced data analytics into the core tracking systems, which will, in turn, help users anticipate disruptions before they impact production schedules.
The deal itself aligns with Bain Capital’s wider strategy of
strengthening its European industrial ecosystems
with targeted investments in both software and physical production, which it believes will create a competitive advantage for its portfolios.
Through our industrials portfolio we are customers of SupplyOn and understand the critical role it plays
Dr Michael Siefke, Partner and Chair of Europe Private Equity at Bain Capital
The combination of digital network capabilities and physical manufacturing assets like Everllence will mean Bain Capital is at the intersection of supply chain operations and will provide it with unique insights into production and distribution challenges.
Dr Michael Siefke, a Partner and Chair of Europe Private Equity at Bain Capital, says: “SupplyOn builds on our long-standing footprint in Europe and Germany specifically.
“Through our industrials portfolio we are customers of SupplyOn and understand the critical role it plays. We have long-standing relationships with the shareholders of SupplyOn and look forward to continuing to support their businesses into the future.”
In terms of numbers, the financial terms of the transaction remain undisclosed, but the investment reflects growing private equity interest in supply chain resilience.
Increasing geopolitical tensions and logistics disruptions have led manufacturing companies to look for increasingly transparent and
automated procurement options
, and the software capabilities of SupplyOn allow companies to monitor supplier performance and manage potential risks in real time.
The previous owners of SupplyOn will keep a minority stake in the company to maintain continuity and support strategic development, which ensures the platform remains deeply rooted in its
core manufacturing sectors
Markus Quicken, Chief Executive Officer at SupplyOn, says: “We are pleased to partner with Bain Capital, which shares our long-term vision for SupplyOn as a strategic asset in the supply chain ecosystem across Europe.”
“This partnership will enable us to accelerate our product roadmap, expand our market reach, and build on the strong foundation our shareholders have created,” says Markus.
The deal, which is expected to finalise before the end of 2026, is subject to customary regulatory approvals. This completion timeline will allow the network to maintain its daily operations without interrupting the procurement activities of its corporate users.
Managing Director

title: BYD unveils its latest luxury EV with sleek fastback styling and 570 miles of range
content:
Peter Johnson
| Jul 21 2026 - 8:43 am PT
revealed the new Denza Z9S, a high-tech luxury electric sedan that can drive over 570 miles (920 km) on a single charge.
BYD’s premium sub-brand, Denza, is welcoming a new member to the family. Denza unveiled the new Z9S on Friday, calling it a “technologically advanced luxury intelligent sedan.”
The electric sedan debuted in a new Dawn Purple color and a sleek fastback design. Up front, the Z9S features BYD’s new dual star-shaped headlights and a closed-off grille design.
Unlike the Denza Z9 GT, which is now on sale in Europe and China, the new Z9S trades the grand-tourer rear end for a fastback coupe silhouette.
Measuring 5,090 mm in length, 1,980 mm in width, and 1,490 mm in height, with a wheelbase of 3,025 mm, the sedan version is slightly smaller and shorter than the Z9 GT (5,180 mm L x 1,990 mm W x 1,500 mm H).
While full details have yet to be revealed, the electric sedan will feature BYD’s next-generation AI-powered Intelligent Cockpit. The system “not only listens, but understands you,” Denza said.
New images released from China’s
reveal the interior for the first time. The Denza Z9S features BYD’s updated cabin design with a three-spoke steering wheel, large floating center infotainment screen, a slim instrument cluster, and physical buttons below the center console.
The seats are redesigned with new headrests and a diamond-pattern design for an added high-quality look. It even includes a separate seat control screen in the rear center armrest.
The Denza Z9S appeared in the batch of new energy vehicles approved for sale earlier this year, revealing several variants offering different power outputs.
A standard version is equipped with a single rear-mounted permanent-magnet synchronous electric motor rated at 429 hp (320 kW). Another single-motor version was listed with slightly more power at up to 496 hp (370 kW).
Both are powered by a 102.326 kWh battery pack, delivering a CLTC range of up to 571 miles (920 km).
While it wasn’t listed in the regulatory filing,
, China’s largest online car-buying hub, lists a tri-motor Denza Z9S variant with nearly 1,200 hp (890 kW) combined.
The new “S” model comes after BYD
launched the Denza Z
at the Goodwood FOS just over a week ago. The Denza Z is the first electric supercar based on BYD’s e3 triple-motor platform. With a combined 1,582 hp and 914 lb-ft of torque, the Denza Z Racing model can accelerate from 0 to 62 mph (0 to 100 km/h) in just 1.96 seconds.
announced last week
that demand for the new electric supercar is “booming,” with global pre-orders exceeding 1,000 in just days.
The Denza Z is available to pre-order in the UK starting at £142,900 ($192,000). In China, it starts at about half that, from 680,000 RMB ($100,000).

title: Tesla's 2026 Summer Update: auto navigation, Grok phone calls, Caraoke scoring, and more
content:
Fred Lambert
| Jul 21 2026 - 8:34 am PT
Tesla started rolling out its 2026 Summer Update, and the automaker published the full release notes on X.
The headline additions: Grok can finally control more of the car, Caraoke now keeps score, navigation learns your routine, and several features move to the mobile app. Here’s every item in the release notes, with some context on each.
Grok can now make phone calls, search and play music, adjust climate, open the glovebox, and answer questions about your Tesla.
This is the piece Grok was missing. When Tesla
first added Grok in July 2025
, the assistant couldn’t actually control any vehicle functions — it was a chatbot bolted onto the screen. Tesla started closing that gap with the
“Hey Grok” wake word in the Spring Update 2026
, and now Grok handles calls, media, climate, and the glovebox by voice.
That’s on top of navigation, which was already added.
Here, Tesla is catching up to most premium automakers who have adopted LLMs within their interfaces, especially in China where the feature is now standard even in cheaper vehicles.
View and share self-driving stats from the mobile app.
Tesla already shows a running tally of miles driven on its “Self-Driving” system in the car. Now those numbers, including streaks and monthly mileage, live in the phone app where owners can screenshot and share them.
Caraoke now scores your singing while in Park. High scores are saved to your Tesla profile.
Caraoke isn’t new. Tesla
added it back in the V10 update in 2019
, and it’s locked to Park for obvious safety reasons. The 2026 version turns it into a game by scoring your performance and saving high scores to your driver profile.
Automatic Navigation now adapts to your routine. In addition to Home, Work, and upcoming calendar events, your vehicle can now suggest and route to places you visit regularly – like a school drop-off on the way to work, or the gym on the way home.
Tesla’s navigation has predicted destinations from Home, Work, and calendar entries for a while. This expands the prediction model to recurring stops it learns from your driving pattern, so the car can offer a route before you enter one.
For more personalized experience, navigation now prioritizes routes that you’ve taken before.
Tied to the same routine-learning idea: when there’s more than one way to get somewhere, the system now leans toward the roads you actually use instead of the default fastest path.
Set your desired Arrival Energy from your phone.
Tesla’s trip planner lets you pick how much battery you want left when you reach a destination — useful for planning charging on longer trips. That setting now moves to the phone app, so you can configure a trip before you get in the car.
Skip the USB drive and upload a custom wrap of your car from the mobile app. Instructions for creating a custom wrap here:
https://github.com/teslamotors/custom-wraps
Tesla lets owners change the color and finish of the 3D car rendering on the screen, a feature it
first opened up for people who wrap their cars in 2022
. Loading a fully custom wrap used to mean copying files onto a USB drive. Now you upload it from the app, and Tesla maintains a public
GitHub repository
with the templates and instructions.
Kids can watch content on the rear screen but only the front row can control it through the rear screen app.
A parental control for cars with a rear screen: back-seat passengers can watch, but only the front row can change what’s playing.
Latest premium Model 3, Model Y, and Cybertruck are all equipped with rear screens.
Tesla also listed a handful of smaller changes in the release notes:
The update is rolling out now. As always, Tesla releases software in waves, so it may take a few weeks to reach every vehicle. You can track versions and features on our
Tesla software updates guide

title: Tesla adds Robotaxi in Tampa and Orlando as Austin stalls at 17 cars
content:
Fred Lambert
| Jul 21 2026 - 7:39 am PT
Tesla switched on its “Robotaxi” service in Tampa and Orlando on Monday, adding two Florida cities to a driverless network that still runs on a couple dozen cars. The company’s @robotaxi account announced it in a four-word post: “Robotaxi now in Tampa & Orlando!”
The timing isn’t subtle. Tesla reports second-quarter earnings on Wednesday, and Robotaxi is the story Elon Musk keeps selling to investors. Two new city names the day before make for a nice slide. The fleet behind them tells a different story.
Tesla didn’t say how many cars it’s putting in Tampa or Orlando. It rarely does. Every market it’s opened beyond Austin this year has launched small, typically with a Tesla employee monitoring from inside the car, and there’s no sign Florida is different.
Robotaxi now in Tampa & Orlando!
Look at Austin, and you can see the ceiling. That’s Tesla’s flagship robotaxi market, the first city it launched in June 2025, and the one with the most built-out service area — Tesla stretched the geofence to cover the entire metro in June. A full year in,
the unsupervised fleet is still stuck at about 17 active cars
, down from a peak of roughly 25 in late April.
Add Dallas and its four cars, and Tesla’s entire unsupervised operation runs on around 21 vehicles, according to community tracking of the fleet. It’s not growing.
We reported in May that the fleet was actually shrinking
, not scaling, and the numbers since then haven’t reversed that.
Here’s the simple version. If Tesla were ready to scale unsupervised robotaxis, it would flood Austin first.
It’s the market with the most data, the most validation, the widest approved service area, and a full year of operation behind it. Every incentive points to putting hundreds of cars there and running a real service. Instead the fleet sits under 20 and drifts sideways, while Tesla lights up new metros in Florida.
Adding cities is cheap.
Lighting up a new metro or redrawing a geofence costs nothing close to what it takes to actually scale a fleet
. It’s a map edit and a tweet. Deploying enough cars to run a dense, reliable service in your best market is the hard part, and that’s the part Tesla hasn’t done anywhere.
The reason isn’t a secret. Musk said it himself.
Q1 2026 earnings call in April
, he named the constraint: “rigorous validation, making sure things are completely safe.” He said Tesla doesn’t want “a single accidental injury” from the rollout (there already were a couple of injuries according to Tesla’s NTHSA reporting). That’s not a manufacturing limit or a software-shipping delay. That’s Tesla keeping the fleet small on purpose because it can’t yet prove the cars are safe enough to scale.
Independent data backs him up. Community tracking of Tesla’s Austin operation has put its incident rate at roughly four times that of human drivers, which is the opposite of what you’d want before adding cars, let alone whole new cities.
The contrast with Waymo is stark. Waymo runs roughly 3,000 driverless vehicles and does more than 500,000 paid trips a week across US cities, and it just
expanded its coverage by more than 20%
. Tesla, after a year, runs about 21 unsupervised cars and just added two markets it isn’t giving a fleet number for.
In short, we are seeing Tesla catching up to Waymo’s service area, but it is doing it with one-hundredth the fleet size. In other words, it is for show.
Adding Tampa and Orlando the day before earnings is a messaging move, not an operational one. Two more city names on the map give Musk something to point to on Tuesday’s call, and they cost Tesla almost nothing to produce.
But the number that matters didn’t move. Tesla’s unsupervised fleet is still under two dozen cars more than a year after launch, in the market it knows best. If the technology were ready to scale, Austin would already be full of driverless Teslas. It isn’t. The fleet is smaller than it was in April.
Musk told us why on the last call: safety validation is the bottleneck, and Tesla won’t take on the injuries that come with putting more cars on the road. That’s the honest answer, and it’s the right call. It’s also completely at odds with a company announcing new cities every couple of weeks as if it’s scaling. You can grow the map or you can grow the fleet. Right now Tesla is only doing one of them, and it’s the easy one. Watch whether anyone on Wednesday’s call asks why.
If you’re relying on a robotaxi someday to cut your driving costs, there’s a bigger win available right now at home: powering your own EV with rooftop solar can drop your fuel bill to nearly nothing. With electricity rates up almost 10% last year and expected to keep climbing, going solar is one of the best ways to protect yourself against rising costs. title: Audi's most affordable EV revealed with over 400 miles of range [Video]
content:
Peter Johnson
| Jul 21 2026 - 7:36 am PT
is bringing back the A2 as an affordable electric car, and it could have one big advantage over the competition. Ahead of its official debut, the EV was spotted with minimal disguise as new details reveal it will deliver more than 400 miles of driving range.
confirming the A2 will return
in all-electric form in March, Audi’s CEO, Gernot Döllner, said the entry-level EV will make “entry into the Audi world easier and more relevant than ever.”
Ahead of its world premiere this fall, Audi revealed prices for the A2 e-tron will start at €38,200 (about $44,000) in Germany with a WLTP driving range of up to 403 miles (649 km).
We also got a better idea of what the final design will look like. While we’ve seen several prototypes out testing, the most recent spy images offer a closer look at Audi’s upcoming EV with hardly any disguise.
An exclusive spy video from
reveals two Audi A2 e-tron prototypes in Copenhagen with minimal camouflage.
The electric A2 takes influence from the original egg-shaped MPV, but with a more modern, crossover hatchback-like design. It features Audi’s new design with a closed-off grille and slim LED headlights.
Although the original egg-shaped design was a bit funny-looking, it was ultra-efficient. The EV retains a similar aerodynamic design to optimize driving range and efficiency.
When the A2 e-tron goes on sale this fall, it will be available with four different power outputs: 125 kW, 140 kW, 170 kW, and 240 kW.
Like the new
Volkswagen ID.3 Neo
, Audi’s entry-level EV is expected to be available with three battery packs: 50 kWh, 58 kWh, and 79 kWh. However, the A2 e-tron will deliver slightly more range at up to 403 miles (649 km).
Starting at €38,200 (about $44,000) in Germany, the A2 e-tron will go on sale this fall as Audi’s most affordable EV. The Q4 e-tron starts at €47,500 ($54,00), or nearly €10,000 more than the electric A2.
Audi said the A2 e-tron will “rejuvenate” the brand. With over 20 new models launched in the past two years, Audi claims it now has the youngest portfolio amongst its rivals.

title: Volkswagen enters the smart e-bike game with radar, cameras, HUD glasses, and more
content:
| Jul 21 2026 - 5:53 am PT
Volkswagen is expanding its presence in the e-bike world with a new lineup of premium electric bicycles developed under license by n+. But instead of chasing ever-bigger motors or batteries, the new models are leaning heavily into automotive-inspired safety technology, packing in an impressive suite of connected features aimed at making urban riding safer.
The main feature grabbing most of the attention from the announcement is what n+ calls Smart View, a radar-assisted rear-view camera system integrated directly into the handlebars. Rather than relying on mirrors or shoulder checks, riders can view live video from behind while simultaneously receiving blind-spot alerts when vehicles approach from the rear. The goal is to keep riders aware of traffic without taking their eyes off the road ahead.
The idea of rear-facing radar isn’t new per se, with systems from Garmin adapting to different bikes and some e-bikes like the
Monarc Tracer
I recently tested offering it as well. But Smart View integrates camera vision as well, giving riders the effect of side/rear view cameras right on their display.
The safety focus continues with an integrated Smart Lights system that goes well beyond a standard headlight and taillight. A full-length LED light strip embedded into the top tube serves as a daytime running light, while also changing color to indicate braking and turn signals. It’s a clever automotive-inspired touch, though n+ notes that some of these lighting functions aren’t currently street legal in every market.
The connected ecosystem extends beyond the bicycle itself. An optional smart helmet synchronizes with the bike’s lighting system and includes crash detection capable of automatically notifying emergency contacts.
Riders can also pair the bike with optional smart glasses that display navigation, blind-spot warnings, and other ride information in a heads-up display positioned within the rider’s field of view. According to n+, the HUD technology comes from engineers who previously developed helmet displays for fighter pilots.
Volkswagen says
the new lineup
reflects its long-standing emphasis on safety, bringing technologies commonly found in passenger vehicles to the rapidly growing micromobility market.
Starting at $3,999 and specced with 250-watt Yamaha mid-drive motors that push the bikes to an EU-legal 25 km/h top speed, they won’t exactly set any impressive lap times or watts-per-dollar records, but these bikes are designed for riders who prioritize ride comfort and quality over speed. Instead of chasing numbers, these bikes seem aimed at riders who prefer designs, features, and technology that improve the everyday usability of the bikes they use to replace cars.
While some of this admittedly sounds like it wandered out of a sci-fi movie, I actually like the direction n+ and Volkswagen are taking here. We’ve spent years watching e-bike marketing focus almost exclusively on bigger motors and higher top speeds. Safety innovation hasn’t always kept pace.
Some of these features may prove more useful than others in real-world riding. A built-in rear-view camera, for example, has the potential to be genuinely useful if implemented well. Blind-spot detection could also be a meaningful upgrade for urban commuters sharing roads with distracted drivers.
I’m a bit more skeptical about the smart glasses. Heads-up displays have promise, but they also risk becoming another distraction if they aren’t executed carefully.
I also wonder how much of this is actually VW, and how much is just n+ lending its e-bikes, as they did with the “Mercedes e-bikes”, for example.
Still, it’s refreshing to see a manufacturer trying something genuinely different. If automotive safety technology can make its way onto e-bikes without making them overly complicated or prohibitively expensive, that’s a trend I’d be happy to see spread throughout the industry.

title: Scientists Restore the Sense of Touch With Electrical Brain Stimulation
content:
Researchers have found that precisely targeted electrical stimulation of the brain can recreate a sense of touch in people with spinal cord injuries.
Researchers at the University of Pittsburgh and the
University of Chicago
followed five people with spinal cord injuries who received implanted brain-computer interface devices. Over a combined 27 years of implant use, the systems delivered about 168 million electrical pulses to the brain without causing any serious adverse events.
“This research plants a flag in the ground for the safety and utility of using brain-computer interfaces to deliver sensory stimulation in clinical settings and, eventually, in people’s homes,” said senior author Robert Gaunt, associate professor of physical medicine and rehabilitation and a member of Pitt’s Rehab Neural Engineering Labs, School of Medicine. “For brain-computer interfaces to have real impact on people’s lives, they need to keep working safely and reliably for years. This study shows that microstimulation in the brain can do exactly that.”
Brain-computer interfaces, or BCIs, translate neural activity into commands that can operate computers, robotic limbs, and other devices. Some systems also work in reverse by sending information into the brain. In this case, tiny electrical pulses were delivered to the somatosensory cortex, the region that processes touch from the body.
That feedback could be crucial for making neuroprosthetic devices more useful. People normally rely on touch to judge pressure, grip strength, texture, and contact. Without it, controlling a robotic hand depends heavily on vision and can require far more concentration.
Pitt’s Rehab Neural Engineering Labs and researchers at the University of Chicago have helped push the field forward for more than a decade. In 2012, a Pitt investigator-led team was among the first to implant electrodes in the motor cortex so a paralyzed person could control a robotic arm. By 2015, the researchers were
the sensory cortex to add a sense of touch. The University of Chicago implanted its first participant with electrodes in both the motor and sensory cortex in 2020.
The five volunteers in the new study allowed researchers to address questions that short experiments cannot answer. They examined whether repeated stimulation caused harmful effects, whether artificial sensations spread to unintended parts of the body, and whether those sensations weakened or changed with time.
The results were encouraging. Electrical pulses aimed at the hand area of the somatosensory cortex continued to produce sensations associated with the hand. Even after years of stimulation, those sensations did not drift to unrelated regions of the body.
Lingering effects were also uncommon. A “persistent sensation” occurred only about once every 23,000 stimulation trials, on average. Most disappeared within 10 seconds after stimulation stopped. None caused pain, and none required medical treatment.
The main limitation was declining electrode performance. Across participants, an average of 64% of the electrodes remained functional. In one person, 60% were still working after 10 years in the brain, although the rate of decline increased later in the study.
“This shows that this technology doesn’t just have to be a short-term solution we test in the lab; industry can start developing long-term take-home solutions for patients,” said lead author Charles Greenspon, an assistant professor in the Department of Neurological Surgery at the University of Chicago.
Similar forms of microstimulation are also being explored in brain regions involved in vision and hearing, raising the possibility that the same basic strategy could eventually help restore several lost senses.
Reference: “Long-term safety and efficacy of intracortical microstimulation in humans” by Charles M. Greenspon, Taylor G. Hobbs, Ceci Verbaarschot, Ali H. Alamri, Natalya D.
